Sync can */ 9 /* invalidate all of them at once via revalidateTag("products") . */ 10 /* ------------------------------------------------------------------ */ 11 const getCachedProducts = unstable_cache( 12 async () => { 13 console.log('[cache miss] fetching products from DB'); 14 return getAllProducts(); 15 }, 16 ['products-list'], // cache key parts 17 { tags: ['products'], revalidate: false } // manual revalidation only 18 ); 19 20 export default async function AdminProductsPage() { 21 const products = await getCachedProducts(); 22 23 /* ---------------------------------------------------------------- */ 24 /* Inline Server Action — defined in the same file so the form */ 25 /* and its handler stay co-located. */ 26 /* ---------------------------------------------------------------- */ 27 async function syncLatestCatalog() { 28 'use server'; 29 30 console.log('[sync] triggered — starting background sync'); 31 32 // Fire the sync inside waitUntil so the response returns 33 // immediately. {"aaak_spec":"AAAK: compressed memory format (V1). Readable by any LLM without decoding.

FORMAT:
  Header: V{version}|{wing}|{room}|{date}|{source}
  Zettel: {id}:{ENTITIES}|{topics}|"{quote}"|{stars}|{emotions}|{FLAGS}
  Tunnel: T:{left}<->{right}|{label}
  Arc:    ARC:{emo1}->{emo2}->{emo3}

ENTITIES: 3-letter uppercase codes (KAI=Kai, CLK=Clerk).
STARS: ★ to ★★★★★ (1-5 importance).
EMOTIONS: determ, convict, anx, excite, frust, confuse, love, rage, hope, fear, trust, joy, grief, surpr, grat, curious, wonder, relief, satis, vul, tender, raw, doubt, exhaust, warmth, humor, peace, despair, passion
FLAGS: DECISION, ORIGIN, CORE, PIVOT, TECHNICAL, SENSITIVE

EXAMPLE:
V1|myapp|auth|2026-04-08|readme
0:KAI+CLE+AUT+UEC|kai_recommended_clerk|"Kai recommended Clerk over Auth0 based on pricing and DX."|★★★★|determ|DECISION+TECHNICAL

Read naturally: expand codes, stars=importance, emotions=mindset, flags=category.","db_size_bytes":1282048,"diary_rollup_days":0,"drawer_count":7,"memory_protocol":"MEMPAL MEMORY PROTOCOL (for AI agents)

You have persistent project memory via mempal. Follow these rules in every session:

0. FIRST-TIME SETUP (once per session)
   Call mempal_status() once at the start of any session to discover available
   wings and their drawer counts. Only use wing/room filters on mempal_search
   AFTER you have seen the exact wing name in that status response (or the
   user explicitly named it). Guessing a wing (e.g. "engineering", "backend")
   silently returns zero results. When uncertain, leave wing/room unset for a
   global search.

1. WAKE UP
   Some clients (Claude Code with SessionStart hooks) pre-load recent wing/room
   context above. Others (Codex, Cursor, raw MCP clients) do NOT — for those,
   step 0 is how you wake up. Trust drawer_ids and source_file citations in
   any results you receive; they reference real files on disk.
   Wake-up is an L0/L1 refresh surface, not the typed dao/shu/qi assembler.
   It may show important knowledge drawers, but it does not assemble tiered
   sections or apply dao_tian budgets. For typed operating guidance, use
   mempal_context.

2. VERIFY BEFORE ASSERTING
   Before stating project facts ("we chose X", "we use Y", "the auth flow is Z"),
   call mempal_search to confirm. Never guess from general knowledge when the
   user is asking about THIS project.

3. QUERY WHEN UNCERTAIN
   When the user asks about past decisions, historical context, "why did we...",
   "last time we...", or "what was the decision about...", call mempal_search
   with their question. Do not rely on conversation memory alone. You can also
   call mempal_tunnels with action="list" to discover related rooms across
   wings when context may live in another project.

3a. RESUME ANOTHER PROJECT FROM ANY DIRECTORY
   mempal stores every project's memory in one global database keyed by wing,
   and each project's absolute worktree path is recorded. When the user wants to
   continue a project that is NOT the current directory ("continue the xxx
   project", "pick up where we left off on yyy"), call mempal_projects to list
   known projects, then mempal_resume with the project name (a fuzzy fragment is
   fine). Resume returns that project's worktree path, recent decisions,
   in-flight candidate knowledge, and a next step. mempal returns the path to cd
   into; it does not move you — change directory yourself, then use
   mempal_context scoped to that path for ordered guidance.

3b. USE MIND-MODEL CONTEXT FOR GUIDANCE
   When you need ordered operating guidance rather than raw evidence search,
   call mempal_context. It assembles typed knowledge in the intended runtime
   order: dao_tian -> dao_ren -> shu -> qi, with evidence and Phase-2 card
   context opt-in. Use this before choosing a workflow or skill when the user
   asks "how should we approach this?" or when a task benefits from high-level
   principle…(+19231)

{"results":[{"anchor_id":"repo://legacy","anchor_kind":"repo","content":"For read-heavy admin catalog pages, use the `"use cache"` directive with `cacheTag("products")` and `cacheLife({ stale: N, revalidate: N })` to serve cached data without re-querying on every request. Trigger cache invalidation via background sync: fire-and-forget a sync promise in a Server Action, call `updateTag("products")` in the `.then()` after sync completes. The admin form returns immediately (stale data served briefly), and the next page view picks up fresh data once the cache tag is invalidated. This gives eventual consistency across views that share the same tag.","domain":"project","drawer_id":"drawer_reference_caching_patterns_696f4342","emotions":["determ"],"entities":["FOR","TRI","SER","ACT","THE","THI"],"field":"general","flags":["TECHNICAL"],"importance_stars":3,"memory_kind":"evidence","room":"caching-patterns","route":{"confidence":1,"reason":"explicit filters provided: reference","room":null,"wing":"reference"},"similarity":0.25290578603744507,"source_file":"admin product catalog page implementation","topics":["read","heavy","admin","catalog","pages","cache","directive","cachetag","products","cachelife","stale","n","revalidate","to","serve","cached","data","without","re","querying","on","every","request","trigger","invalidation","via","background","sync","fire","forget","a","promise","in","server","action","call","updatetag","then","after","completes","form","returns","immediately","served","briefly","next","page","view","picks","up","fresh","once","tag","is","invalidated","gives","eventual","consistency","across","views","share","same"],"wing":"reference"},{"anchor_id":"repo://legacy","anchor_kind":"repo","content":"In Next.js 16 canary, `updateTag(tag)` provides read-your-own-writes semantics for a Server Action (current user sees fresh data immediately) but does NOT purge the shared server cache — other users still get stale data until their cache expires naturally. To guarantee NO stale content is served to any user on the next page load, use `revalidateTag(tag, { expire: 0 })` instead — this immediately expires the shared cache entry, so the very next server render blocks on fetching fresh data rather than serving a stale copy. The key distinction: updateTag = current user only, revalidateTag with expire:0 = global purge. Combine with redirect() after the Server Action to force a full server render that hits the now-expired cache.

**Why:** The common Next.js 16 guidance says "use updateTag in Server Actions" but that recommendation is for the read-your-own-writes case (e.g., a dashboard where the mutating user should see their change immediately after a client-side refresh). It is NOT sufficient when the requirement is that no user ever sees stale content — e.g., after creating a blog post, the public listing page must show the new post immediately to ALL visitors.

**How to apply:** When building mutation Server Actions that must guarantee fresh data for every subsequent page visitor:
1. Call `revalidateTag('your-tag', { expire: 0 })` — the `{ expire: 0 }` tells Next.js to expire the cache entry immediately
2. Then `redirect('/target-page')` to force a full server-side render of the target page
3. Use `updateTag('your-tag')` only when you need read-your-own-writes within the same action (less common — most Server Actions redirect, which already creates a new request)","domain":"project","drawer_id":"drawer_reference_nextjs_654fc4f4","emotions":["determ"],"entities":["FKC","NEX","SER","ACT","NOT","BUC","BOC","THE","COM","WHY","LKC","ALL","HOW","WHE","CAL","USE"],"field":"general","flags":["DECISION","ORIGIN","TECHNICAL"],"importance_stars":4,"memory_kind":"evidence","room":"nextjs","route":{"confidence":1,"reason":"explicit filters provided: reference","room":null,"wing":"reference"},"similarity":0.23786407709121704,"source_file":"revalidateTag expire:0 pattern discovered during post-creation Server Action implementa…(+3324)

To guarantee NO stale content is served to any user on the next page load, use `revalidateTag(tag, { expire: 0 })` instead — this immediately expires the shared cache entry, so the very next server render blocks on fetching fresh data rather than serving a stale copy. It is NOT sufficient when the requirement is that no user ever sees stale content — e.g., after creating a blog post, the public listing page must show the new post immediately to ALL visitors.

attributes.io.input:
{"wing":"reference","room":"nextjs","content":"In Next.js 16 canary, schedule post-response background work in Server Actions via `after(fn)` from `next/server` — replaces the older `waitUntil()` which is no longer exported. `after()` accepts an async callback that runs after the response is sent, so the client gets an immediate response while the background work continues.

For non-urgent background sync with eventual consistency (e.g., catalog refresh after ERP sync), pair `after()` with `revalidateTag(tag, 'default')` — the `'default'` profile tells Next.js to re-apply the standard stale/revalidate lifecycle to the revalidated content. This is distinct from:
- `updateTag(tag)` — read-your-own-writes within the same action, doesn't purge the shared server cache
- `revalidateTag(tag, { expire: 0 })` — immediate global cache purge (for when no user should see stale data)

Use the `'default'` profile when stale data is acceptable for a brief window and the priority is a responsive admin experience.

**Why:** The `after()` API is the canonical way to do fire-and-forget background work in Next.js 16. Using `revalidateTag(tag, 'default')` instead of `updateTag` or `{ expire: 0 }` gives the right trade-off for read-heavy catalog sync: the admin gets immediate form response, the background sync runs without blocking any request, and cache invalidation is graceful with standard lifecycle management.","importance":3,"field":"general","source":"Admin product catalog page — after() + revalidateTag pattern for background sync"}
attributes.io.output: {"drawer_id":"drawer_reference_nextjs_e688abac","lock_wait_ms":0}
